attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman is admitted to hospital for right shoulder arthroscopy. She has no significant medical history, takes no regular medications and has never had any type of anaesthetic before. In the anaesthetic bay, she is positioned for an interscalene brachial plexus block and 20 mL of ropivacaine is injected. Within two minutes, she reports feeling strange.

On examination, she appears anxious. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 150/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She is tremulous and reports perioral tingling, ringing in her ears, and a metallic taste in her mouth.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for these symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario describes the early signs of local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) following an interscalene block. The rapid onset of symptoms (within two minutes) and the constellation of perioral tingling, tinnitus, and metallic taste strongly suggest intravascular injection of local anaesthetic.

Intravascular injection of local anaesthetic typically presents with central nervous system symptoms first, followed by cardiovascular symptoms. Early recognition is crucial as it can progress to seizures and cardiovascular collapse. The progression typically follows: perioral numbness, metallic taste, visual/auditory disturbances → agitation, confusion, muscle twitching → seizures → CNS depression → cardiovascular collapse.

An anxiety attack may present with similar autonomic features (tachycardia, hypertension) but would not typically cause perioral tingling or metallic taste. While anxiety is common before surgery, these specific symptoms point to a different cause, and anxiety should be a diagnosis of exclusion.

An allergic reaction to local anaesthetic would typically present with urticaria, bronchospasm, or hypotension rather than the CNS symptoms described. True allergic reactions to amide local anaesthetics are extremely rare.

Pneumothorax is a potential complication of interscalene block but would present with chest pain, dyspnoea, and decreased breath sounds on the affected side. The CNS symptoms described would not be present.

A vasovagal episode typically presents with bradycardia, hypotension, pallor, and sweating - the opposite of the sympathetic activation seen here. The specific sensory symptoms would not be present.

Management of LAST includes stopping the injection, calling for help, managing the airway, treating seizures if present, and considering lipid emulsion therapy if cardiovascular collapse occurs. Prevention strategies include careful aspiration before injection, incremental dosing, and use of ultrasound guidance.
learningPoints: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) presents with initial CNS excitation followed by depression, and cardiovascular collapse may occur.
Early recognition of perioral numbness, metallic taste, and visual/auditory disturbances is crucial in preventing progression to seizures and cardiac arrest.
Intravascular injection during regional anaesthesia is a common cause of LAST and can be minimised with aspiration and incremental dosing.
references: Shalaby M, Sahni R, Hamilton R. Local anesthetic systemic toxicity: awareness, recognition, and risk mitigation in the emergency department. Clin Exp Emerg Med. 2024 Jun;11(2):121-126. doi: 10.15441/ceem.24.231. Epub 2024 May 23. PMID: 38778495; PMCID: PMC11237253.

Mahajan A, Derian A. Local Anesthetic Toxicity. [Updated 2022 Oct 3]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499964/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
